coredata.dc:title: Functional enhancement of laser deposited carbon-based supercapacitor electrodes upon post-annealing treatment

coredata.dc:description: The development of new synthetic methods is paramount for the versatile production of complex multicomponent electrodes for supercapacitors with enhanced performance. The reactive inverse matrix assisted pulsed laser evaporation (RIMAPLE) technique shows promise for the deposition of complex functional nanocomposites in a facile way. In this work, hybrid supercapacitor electrodes constituted by reduced graphene oxide (rGO) and carbon nanotubes (CNTs) decorated with a myriad of cerium and manganese oxide nanoparticles were obtained by using GO sheets, CNTs, CeO2 nanoparticles and, for the first time, the (NH4)2[Mn2(C6H5O7)2(H2O)2] coordination compound as precursors in the RIMAPLE targets. Compositional and structural characterizations revealed that post-annealing treatments at mild conditions (150-250 °C) induce a further reduction of the rGO and CNTs, besides an oxidation of the Ce oxide phases (Ce3+ to Ce4+) and a reduction of the Mn oxides (Mn3+ to Mn2+). The substantial change of the carbon and metal oxide nanostructures causes an up to 8-fold increase of the capacitance of the electrodes (63 F/cm3 @ 10 mV/s). Finally, the generation of a high quantity of edge defects at 250 °C and long dwell time leads to the drop of the electrode's capacitance.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Absorbance spectra of the coordination complex solution used as matrix in the MAPLE targets and a 1:10 diluted solution. Inset: photograph of a typical solution.

(a) XHRSEM images of the samples annealed with 250 °C during 2 h and 6 h. (b) HAADF STEM images of the as deposited film as well as the sample annealed during 4 h at 150 °C. The outer walls of two MWCNTs are also indicated with dashed lines. (c) HRTEM images of crystals in as the deposited film and the annealed film at 250 °C during 6 h. Insets: FFT filtered images of the indicated regions.
Typical XPS C1s, O1s, Ce3d and Mn2p high resolution spectra and the areal percentage of the deconvoluted peaks for all the samples.
(a) Typical Raman spectrum with deconvoluted bands, (b) average D/G and D/D′ intensity ratios of the obtained samples, evolution of (c) D/G and (d) D/D′ intensity ratios as a function of FWHM(G).

(a) Volumetric capacitance versus sweep rate of the electrodes characterized through cyclic voltammetry. Insets: CV plots of the as deposited electrode and the one annealed at 250 °C during 4 h. (b) Maximum capacitance (measured @ 10 mV/s) versus annealing time. (c) Volumetric capacitance versus current density measured in galvanostatic charge-discharge mode. Insets: typical charge-discharge curves acquired at 1 mA/cm2 of the as deposited electrode and the one annealed at 250 °C during 4 h. (d) Volumetric capacitance versus discharge time. (e) Nyquist plot of all the electrodes. Insets: Nyquist plot zoomed to the highest frequency range and equivalent electric circuit used for the fitting of the plots.
Calculated areal percentage of the Mn oxidation states.
Calculated LD and nD of the as deposited and annealed films.
Fitted parameters of the proposed equivalent circuit.

head.ce:title: Functional enhancement of laser deposited carbon-based supercapacitor electrodes upon post-annealing treatment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of new synthetic methods is paramount for the versatile production of complex multicomponent electrodes for supercapacitors with enhanced performance. The reactive inverse matrix assisted pulsed laser evaporation (RIMAPLE) technique shows promise for the deposition of complex functional nanocomposites in a facile way. In this work, hybrid supercapacitor electrodes constituted by reduced graphene oxide (rGO) and carbon nanotubes (CNTs) decorated with a myriad of cerium and manganese oxide nanoparticles were obtained by using GO sheets, CNTs, CeO2 nanoparticles and, for the first time, the (NH4)2[Mn2(C6H5O7)2(H2O)2] coordination compound as precursors in the RIMAPLE targets. Compositional and structural characterizations revealed that post-annealing treatments at mild conditions (150-250 °C) induce a further reduction of the rGO and CNTs, besides an oxidation of the Ce oxide phases (Ce3+ to Ce4+) and a reduction of the Mn oxides (Mn3+ to Mn2+). The substantial change of the carbon and metal oxide nanostructures causes an up to 8-fold increase of the capacitance of the electrodes (63 F/cm3 @ 10 mV/s). Finally, the generation of a high quantity of edge defects at 250 °C and long dwell time leads to the drop of the electrode's capacitance.

ce:section.ce:para: The urgent transition to a more sustainable society based on electricity rather than fossil fuels requires the development of high-power energy storage systems. Electrochemical capacitors, also known as supercapacitors (SCs), stand out as high-power devices with fast charge and discharge times that, nevertheless, can store limited energy as compared to batteries [1]. Therefore, intense scientific and technologic efforts are focused on increasing the energy density of SCs maintaining high power density. For achieving this goal, the electrode materials are mainly requested to exhibit high electric conductivity, high surface area, high porosity allowing fast electrolyte ion diffusion, as well as structural-chemical stability. Activated carbon electrodes, used in commercial SCs, allow the production of high-power devices but with modest energy densities. Remarkably, nanocarbon-based (i.e. carbon nanotube (CNT) and graphene) electrodes show great promise for enhancing the SCs performance due to their inherent high electric conductivity and surface area. In addition, the decoration of the nanocarbons with pseudocapacitive entities (functional groups, metal oxides, polymers) has been proven as an encouraging strategy to significantly increase the capacitance of the electrodes [2,3]. However, the use of these materials in industrial applications is facing obstacles due to synthesis complications for obtaining the required structural-functional properties at low-moderate costs due to their tendency to aggregate in dispersions, difficulties for attaining controlled doping and decoration with functional groups, synthetic/chemical incompatibilities, etc [4–6]. Therefore, new solutions should be investigated for providing radical improvements and creating new industrial opportunities. Graphene oxide (GO) is an insulating material that can be considered as a graphene sheet decorated with a disordered plethora of nanoregions comprised of oxygen-containing functional groups (epoxide, hydroxide, carbonyl, carboxyl …), in addition to impurities (atoms of nitrogen, sulphur …) and crystalline defects [7,8]. Importantly, the hydrophilic and reactive nature of GO allow the synthesis of relatively complex graphene-derivative dispersions and ease their processability. As well, it has been extensively proven the reduction process of GO after its thermal annealing or light illumination leading to a conductive material with graphene-like structure (reduced GO, rGO) [9,10].

A plethora of novel technologies are being continuously developed for achieving the synthesis of nanocarbon materials with enhanced functional properties and in scalable way [11]. Among all of them, laser processing methods are attracting high interest lately. Laser processing of materials is characterized by their exposure to fast and confined energy. The special characteristics of laser-based methods are providing ground-breaking advances for many technological areas, including energy storage [10]. Since localized laser irradiation can induce very intense heating, besides photochemical processes, the development of several coupled mechanisms can be driven, promoting the atomic mobility, the creation of defects and the subsequent structural modification in the irradiated materials [12–14]. As an example, laser-induced growth of inorganic nanostructures on CNTs and rGO surfaces was reported by direct laser irradiation of mixed nanocarbon and precursor materials leading to the formation of hybrid compounds [15–20]. Beyond heating, the extreme thermodynamic conditions driven by intense laser radiation can create ablation processes in carbon materials [12,21–23], which can be used for the advanced synthesis of nanocarbon-based materials. Special relevance is devoted to pulsed laser deposition (PLD) technique, in which a solid target is irradiated with intense laser pulses for inducing its fast surface ablation. The laser-ablated species exhibit high kinetic energy (up to a few keV) and, thus, their high mobility allows growing adherent thin films with high crystallinity. Though high vacuum is necessary, this method is simple, fast, permits a good control of the morphology and thickness, and was demonstrated to be adaptable to industrial processes. The PLD growth of graphene onto different types of substrates was accomplished by means of the ablation of pyrolytic carbon, graphite and HOPG targets with and without the presence of metal catalysts as Ni, Cu, Co, Sn, Fe and Cu–Ni alloys [24,25]. Furthermore, the growth of N-doped graphene thin film layers was achieved by the PLD method with the presence of nitrogen gas during the ablation process [26,27]. Ablation processes are also conducted in liquid media for obtaining graphene-based materials. Bilayer and few layer graphene nanowalls, besides carbon nanoparticles (NPs) were obtained by laser ablation of graphite targets immersed in liquids as water or dimethyformamide [28–30]. Graphene quantum dots were also synthesized by pulsed laser ablation of carbon nano-onions immersed in distilled water [31–34]. In view of its simplicity and versatility, the ablation of carbon sources in reactive environments is foreseen as a valuable methodology for obtaining a wide variety of doped graphene derivatives.

Besides PLD and direct laser ablation of carbon materials, a new approach uses the matrix assisted pulsed laser evaporation (MAPLE) technique for carrying out the controlled deposition of nanocarbon entities avoiding their decomposition. This methodology is similar to PLD, although frozen dispersions containing the nanoentities are used as targets. The laser radiation can be either absorbed directly by the frozen solvent (matrix) or by the dispersed carbon nanoentities (inverse MAPLE) leading to the explosive boiling of the matrix in a rapid and energetic way. The generated vapour carries the nanoentities towards a facing substrate leading to their gentle deposition in form of nanocarbon films. With this approach, thin coatings constituted by single wall CNTs functionalized with carboxylic groups, multiwall CNTs, and GO sheets were deposited in controlled way [35–37]. The transferred CNTs maintained their structure, exhibiting low amount of laser-induced defects when irradiated with moderate laser fluences, whereas the GO sheets underwent reduction processes and even N-doping upon their deposition in nitrogen gas ambient accounting for reactive processes. Furthermore, hybrid thin films composed of rGO decorated with metal and transition metal oxide NPs were also fabricated through irradiation of frozen dispersions containing GO flakes, NPs and even organic/inorganic precursors for attaining the N-doping of rGO [19,38–41]. This methodology, named reactive inverse MAPLE (RIMAPLE), allows the fabrication of functional thin films on any substrate and at room temperature in adaptable and controlled way. In this case, simultaneous deposition and chemical transformation of the nanoentities is carried out for enhancing the electrodes’ performance. Particularly, this technique was leveraged for the deposition of graphene-based SC hybrid electrodes. Flexible composite electrodes constituted by N-doped rGO and CNTs decorated with transition metal Ni, Ce and Mn oxides nanostructures were obtained by UV laser irradiation of frozen aqueous targets containing GO flakes, transition metal oxide NPs, metal organic precursors, and nitrogen-containing molecular compounds [42–46].

It is worth noticing the great flexibility of the MAPLE routes for the synthesis of complex compounds and the activation of complex physicochemical mechanisms between the reactants during the deposition process by just modifying the composition of the target. However, it has to be remarked that the use of complex compounds as precursors or the effect of post-annealing treatments on the structural and functional properties of the electrodes have not been investigated yet. These are the specific objectives of this work, in which the deposition of hybrid electrodes composed of rGO and CNTs coated with cerium and manganese oxides was accomplished by using the Mn (II) citrate complex (NH4)2[Mn2(C6H5O7)2(H2O)2] in the MAPLE target as precursor for the crystallization of manganese oxides. Additionally, mild heating processes in air were conducted to induce structural-compositional modifications which significantly affect the energy storage performance of the electrodes.

The solutions used in the synthesis of the ammonium/manganese/citrate/water (NH4)2[Mn2(C6H5O7)2(H2O)2] coordination complex precursor were prepared in the open air. The chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification. The synthesis route of the compound was based on bibliographic references [47,48] with some modifications. In 6 mL of deionized water, 0.1 g of Mn (II) acetate (Mn(CH₃CO₂)₂; 0.58 mmol, Sigma-Aldrich, ≥99.5%) was dissolved with 0.2 g of citric acid (C₆H₈O₇; 1.04 mmol, Sigma-Aldrich, ≥99.5%), by continuous stirring for 2 h at 30 °C. Next, 1 mL of aqueous ammonia (NH3; 30% vol, Sigma-Aldrich) was carefully added in order to create the (NH4)2[Mn2(C6H5O7)2(H2O)2] coordination complex and adjust the pH of the mixture to 8. During the synthesis procedure, the mixture was subjected to three sequential stirring processes: i) continuous stirring for 1 h at 50 °C, ii) 5 h stirring at a temperature of 30 °C, and iii) the mixture was brought to room temperature, stirred for 8 h, and settled for 4 days to stabilize.

For the preparation of the MAPLE targets, 4.45 g of the prepared solution was taken. 250 mg of GO powder (Nanoinnova Technologies), 250 mg of CeO2 nanoparticles (Sigma-Aldrich), and 50 mg of multiwall CNTs (MWCNT) doped with carboxylic groups (Sigma-Aldrich) were added to the solution to obtain 5 g of the final dispersion. The GO powder was composed of 1–15 layers’ thick flakes of about 1 μm [2] lateral size. The mean size of the CeO2 NPs was ca. 25 nm, and the carbon nanotubes were about 10 nm in diameter and up to 1.5 μm in length. Once prepared, the dispersions were magnetically stirred for about 30 min and sonicated at room temperature for 1 min. The MAPLE targets were prepared by filling aluminium holders of 3 mL and subsequently flash frozen by their immersion in liquid-N2. Lastly, the targets were sited inside the deposition chamber, remaining frozen during the deposition process by means of a liquid N2-cooled holder. The temperature of the frozen target was kept at ca. −80 °C in order to avoid the sublimation of the water solvent in the vacuum conditions. The electrodes were deposited by using a Quantel Brilliant B Nd:YAG laser system working at 266 nm wavelength (4th harmonic). 6000 laser pulses (ca. 4 ns pulse duration, 10 Hz repetition rate, 0.4 J/cm2 laser fluence) were submitted to the frozen targets. The surface morphology of the frozen target is key for obtaining a homogeneous deposition. Hence, the laser beam was scanned over the target surface at an incident angle of 45° during the deposition process in order to avoid the creation of a deep crater so that maintaining the generated plasma oriented towards the substrate, leading to a steady deposition rate. 0.1 mm-thick stainless steel AISI 316 L substrates, 1 × 1 cm2 in size, were placed in front of the targets at a separation distance of 4 cm. After an evacuation to a residual pressure of 0.1 Pa, the deposition process was performed in a 20 Pa N2 background gas environment and at room temperature. This procedure for the preparation of the target and the deposition of the films is the result of a thorough optimization process. Additional thermal annealing treatments in air were submitted to the deposited electrodes by means of a Memmert UN30plus stove. The treatments were carried out during 2 h, 4 h and 6 h at both 150 °C and 250 °C, for studying the influence of the respective dwell time and the temperature on the structural and functional properties of the electrodes.

UV–Vis–NIR absorbance spectra of the coordination complex solution were acquired in the 200–1300 nm wavelength range by means of a Jasco V-770 spectrophotometer. The morphology of the deposited films was investigated with an extreme high resolution electron microscope (XHRSEM) Magellan 400 L (FEI). The analysis of the materials’ structure was carried out with high-angle annular dark field (HAADF) imaging by high resolution scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) as well as with high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) by using a FEI Tecnai F20 equipment. The surface chemical composition of the obtained electrodes was studied by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) by means of a SPECS XPS spectrometer based on a Phoibos 150 electron energy analyser operating in a constant energy mode and using an aluminium anode (Al Kα 1486.74 eV) as a monochromatic X-ray source. Wide range spectra over 1500 eV binding energies were recorded using a 50 eV analyser pass energy. Additional high-resolution spectra were also obtained over 20 eV ranges at 10 eV pass energy with an energy resolution of 0.7 eV. All the XPS measurements were performed in ultra-high vacuum (≈10−7 Pa). Raman spectra were recorded with a LABRAM (HORIBA) spectrometer at 532 nm and 638 nm wavelengths with a laser power 2 mW focused to a spot size of ca. 0.7 μm. Two consecutive measurements with 10 s acquisition time were performed for increasing the signal-to-noise ratio and eliminating the spikes created by cosmic rays.

The electrochemical properties of the synthesized electrodes were studied with a Keithley 2450-EC Electrochemistry Lab System. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) studies were performed in the [0, 0.8] V potential range and over a sweep rate range of 10–150 mV/s. The films were used as working electrodes in a three-electrode configuration using a plate material electrochemical cell (Bio-logic), and Ag/AgCl reference electrode (3 M KCl internal solution), a Pt wire counter electrode and 1 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution as electrolyte at room temperature. The samples’ analyzed area in the electrochemical cell was 0.5 cm2. Galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) investigations were carried out with the same cell configuration but varying the current density in the 0.08–12 mA/cm2 range. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were also carried out by using a Hioki IM3590 chemical impedance analyser. Impedance values were acquired in the frequency range 1 Hz–200 kHz at open circuit potential with an amplitude perturbation of 5 mV. An average of 5 measurements per data point was obtained to minimize the experimental error. The regression of the EIS data to the equivalent circuit models was made with the EIS Spectrum Analyser software.

The dynamics of the laser-induced evaporation process that leads to the deposition of the films depend in a high degree on the optical properties of the matrix. In the RIMAPLE process, the water ice matrix is highly transparent to the UV laser radiation and the nanocarbon and metal oxide NPs become the absorbing entities that heat up the surrounding water ice, inducing its explosive boiling [38,46]. The optical absorption of the (NH4)2[Mn2(C6H5O7)2(H2O)2] solution used as matrix in this work was studied by means of UV–Vis–NIR spectrophotometry (Fig. 1 ). The plots reveal a significant absorbance at the laser wavelength (266 nm), resulting an optical penetration depth of ca. 12 μm into the matrix. Therefore, besides the main heating contribution from the GO sheets, MWCNTs and CeO2 NPs, the matrix is also directly heated and evaporated upon the direct action of the laser pulses. It is worth noticing that the coordination bond energy of Mn+2 citrate complexes is about 0.1 eV, much lower than the laser photon energy (4.7 eV) [49], pointing to the generation of photochemical mechanisms. As a consequence, the absorbed energy leads to the simultaneous deposition of the carbon and CeO2 nanostructures, as well as the decomposition of the coordinated compound into high energy ionic species, generating a very rich chemical and reactive plasma, which leads to the deposition of a composite with complex structure.

The deposited films, about 1–2 μm in thickness, show a black colour that do not change after the annealing treatments. The XHRSEM inspection discloses a heterogeneous mixture of randomly oriented MWCNTs and GO sheets covered by nanometre-sized NPs (Fig. 2 a). Voids and channels of about tens to two hundred nanometres in size reveal a highly porous structure, which would promote the fast electrolyte ionic diffusion to the electrode's surface (i.e. allowing high charge-discharge power). A low number of spherical and irregular aggregates, up to ca. 200 nm in size, also appear unevenly distributed. These aggregates are associated to CeO2 NPs enduring melting-coalescence-recrystallization mechanisms during the laser irradiation of the targets. No significant morphological changes are detected after the thermal annealing treatments. TEM characterization was carried out for a high-resolution study of the materials structure. HAADF STEM images of as deposited films as well as post-annealed samples show the formation of metal oxide NPs of up to tens of nm decorating the surface of the carbon nanostructures (Fig. 2b). The largest NPs show polygonal shape, whereas the smallest ones disclose spherical-irregular shape and also appear in the inner cavities of the MWCNTs. Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (TEM-EDX; not shown) display the presence of Ce and Mn in the same regions, and even in the same particles in various cases, pointing to a great mixture of Ce and Mn oxides. The large polygonal NPs mainly show the presence of Ce, and they are accordingly ascribed to Ce oxides. High-resolution TEM analyses reveal no substantial amorphization of the MWCNTs and the formation of highly crystalline NPs of multiple phases {α-CeO2 (JCPDS 00-004-0593), Ce2O3 (JCPDS 00-023-1048), CeO (JCPDS 00-033-0334), α-MnO2 (JCPDS 00-053-0633), Mn3O4 (JCPDS 01-075-0765), Mn2O3 (JCPDS 01-071-0636)}. Fig. 2c shows two examples of NPs in as deposited and annealed (6 h at 250 °C) samples. The fast Fourier transformed (FFT) images reveal mainly four families of planes with specific interplane distances: 0.29 nm and 0.32 nm, appearing in the pyramid-like crystal of the image of the as deposited film and in the “region 1” of the annealed one. These planes could be respectively attributed to (101) Ce2O3 (100% of normalized intensity in powder standard)/(111) CeO (100%)/(110) Mn3O4 (10%) and (310) or strained (110) MnO2 (70%/13%). 0.20 nm and 0.34 nm planes are also detected, constituting the large NP in the image of the as deposited sample and the “region 2” of the annealed one, which could be ascribed to (110) Ce2O3 (35%)/(220) CeO2 (51%)/(332) Mn2O3 (9%) and (100) Ce2O3 (30%)/(022) Mn3O4 (7%), respectively. It has to be noted that these interplane distance values differ a little bit in some cases from the tabulated ones, and edge dislocations are also identified in many selected areas filtered by FFT, indicating the development of high strain in the crystals due to the extremely large temperatures, gradients and growth rates during the crystallization processes.

The surface chemical composition of the as deposited sample, as well as the annealed ones during 4 h at 150 °C and 4 h, 6 h at 250 °C was characterized through XPS, allowing the determination of the functional groups contained in the carbon structures and the oxidation state of the Ce and Mn cations in the NPs. Fig. 3 presents typical high resolution spectra of the C1s, O1s, Ce3d and Mn2p orbitals, including the deconvoluted peaks as well as their areal percentage that accounts for their relative quantity. Despite the presence of NH4 + groups in the coordinated complex precursor as well as the background N2 gas, and contrarily to previous works using other nitrogen containing precursors [42,43,45,50], N1s signal was not detected in any of the films pointing to the absence of N-doping of the carbon nanostructures. C1s spectra were deconvoluted in four peaks centred at 284.9, 285.9, 286.9 and 289.0 eV, respectively ascribed to CC sp2 (probably with some contribution of C–C sp3), hydroxyl (COH), epoxy (COC) and carboxylic (COOH) chemical groups [42,45]. As observed from their areal percentage, the thermal annealing increases the relative amount of sp2 CC bonds, indicating a reduction process of the rGO sheets and MWCNTs, to some extent accompanied with a restoration of the graphitic structure, that augments with temperature and annealing time. Moreover, the quantity of COH seems to decrease in a faster rate than COC and COOH functional groups at long dwell times. O1s spectra were deconvoluted into Mn–O–Mn, Ce4+-O, Ce3+-O (also associated to oxygen vacancies O2− in the lattice of the metal oxide), OH and COOH peaks located at 529.5, 530.2, 321.4, 532.2 and 533.3 eV, respectively [51–55]. As witnessed, the thermal annealing induces the increase of Mn–O and Ce–O signals due to the decline of C–O functionalities. The Mn–O rise (ca. × 6) is proportionally greater than the Ce–O one (ca. × 3). The study of the Ce oxidation states was carried out through the deconvolution of the Ce 3d3/2 and 3d5/2 signals. Six contributions labelled as v0, v1, v2 (3d5/2) and v’0, v’1, v’2 (3d3/2) associated to Ce4+ spin–orbit doublets, as well as u0, u’0 and u1, u’2 related to Ce3+ spin–orbit doublets were identified [56]. No Ce2+ signal was detected, discarding the formation of CeO. Consequently, part of the initial CeO2 NPs contained in the MAPLE target became reduced after the laser-induced melting and recrystallization processes. Since the samples also contain Mn, the Ce 3d3/2 line overlaps with the Mn LMM Auger line (900 eV), and the calculation of Ce4+ cannot be carried out through the areal percentage of the deconvoluted peaks. Therefore, the amount of Ce4+ was assessed with the percentage of v’2 peak area respect to the total Ce 3d area using Eq. (1):

being %v’2 the percentage of v’2 peak area respect to the total Ce 3d area [57]. An increase of the Ce oxidation state is observed with the annealing process, increasing the percentage of Ce4+ from ca. 59% in the as deposited film to about 96–97% in those annealed at 250 °C. Accordingly, thermal annealing may transform Ce2O3 and CeO2-x into CeO2. Finally, the Mn2p spectra show a Mn 2p1/2 and Mn 2p3/2 spin orbit doublet, with a separation of about 12 eV in all the cases. The Mn 2p3/2 line was also deconvoluted in characteristic peaks located at 641.5, 642.5, 643.8 and 645.9 eV associated to Mn2+, Mn3+, Mn4+ and a satellite, respectively [58,59]. These results would account for the formation of MnO, Mn3O4, Mn2O3 and MnO2. It is worth noting that previous works using Mn(II) acetate as precursor in the RIMAPLE target did not allow to form MnO2 phases [46]. As observed, the areal percentage of Mn2+ tends to increase with the thermal annealing at the expense of Mn3+, remaining almost unaltered the Mn4+ percentage (once discarded the satellite contribution; Table 1 ). This result may point to a main reduction process of Mn2O3 to Mn3O4/MnO, and/or Mn3O4 to MnO especially at the sample annealed during 6 h at 250 °C, being the relative amount of MnO2 unaltered. Summarizing, the thermal treatment causes the simultaneous reduction of the rGO sheets and MWCNTs, the oxidation of Ce2O3 and CeO2-x into CeO2, and the reduction of the Mn3+ oxide species (Mn2O3 to Mn3O4/MnO, and/or Mn3O4 to MnO). These phase transformations are more noticeable in the annealing processes carried out at 250 °C, especially after 6 h of treatment.

Raman spectroscopy characterization was used for the diagnosis of the damage created in the carbon nanostructures (rGO, MWCNTs) upon the post-annealing process. The spectra of the samples were fitted with three main bands located at 1340 cm−1 (D), 1580 cm−1 (G), and 1614 cm−1 (D′) besides two additional broad bands at 1200 cm−1 (I), and 1490 cm−1 (D″) (Fig. 4 a). The G band is related to the sp2-type carbon bonds, whereas the D band is ascribed to an activation of the A1g breathing mode of the hexagonal rings in the graphene containing structural defects such as vacancies, edges, or sp3-bonds [17,42]. The D/G intensity ratio (ID/IG) was used to estimate the variation of the population of structural defects caused by annealing. Apart of that, the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the G band was also recorded, since it always rises with the structural disorder. The D and G band in the spectra shown in Fig. 4a are relatively narrow and fall in the regime of nano-crystalline graphene [60]. As observed in Fig. 4b, the average D/G ratio of as deposited and annealed samples is similar (1.7–1.8). However, variations of the D/G ratio versus the G band bandwidth are observed in the spectra taken on different locations. Fig. 4c shows an evolution of D/G as a function of the FWHM(G) which depends on annealing temperature and dwell time. Note that there is a certain dispersion of the D/G ratio across the surface which is attributed to variations of the photochemical processes during the target irradiation. The as deposited film as well as the annealed sample after 4 h at 150 °C and 250 °C show an increasing trend of D/G with the FWHM(G), accounting for a low defect density. The sample annealed at 250 °C during 6 h shows the largest values of FWHM(G) (i.e. the largest structural disorder) and a decreasing D/G, indicating a high defect density. The average distance between defects (LD) as well as the corresponding density of defects (nD) were calculated using Eqs. (2) and (3) and Eqs. (4) and (5) for low and high density of defects regimes [60,61], respectively.

In these equations, EL refers to the photon energy (eV) used in the Raman characterization. The obtained values, summarized in Table 2 , reveal a similar average distance between defects (around 11 nm) in the as deposited sample and those annealed during 4 h at both 150 °C and 250 °C, whereas the sample annealed during 6 h at 250 °C presents a substantial reduction of LD to 1.4 nm. Accordingly, the films exhibit a relatively low density of defects (about 3 × 1011 cm−2), being about two orders of magnitude higher at the sample annealed at 250 °C during 6 h. The nature of the defects present in the films was deduced from the D/D′ intensity ratio [62,63]. As seen in Fig. 4b, the average D/D′ ratio is about 3.0–3.5 in all the films, accounting for the formation of edge defects. Interestingly, the D/D′ ratio decreases with increasing FWHM(G) in all the annealed films, reaching the lowest values in the sample treated at 250 °C during 6 h, while the D/D′ ratio appears rather invariable in the as deposited sample. These results would effectively point to the main creation of larger quantity of edge defects in the rGO sheets and MWCNTs upon annealing treatment.

The functional properties of all the produced samples were tested using them as working electrodes in electrochemical experiments in the 0–0.8 V potential window. First of all, the performance of the positrodes was studied through cyclic voltammetry in the 10–150 mV/s sweep rate range. The CVs show the typical rectangular-like profile without prominent redox peaks, denoting a capacitive nature of the electrodes with a main contribution of the surface charge storage mechanisms (Fig. 5 a) [64,65]. The volumetric capacitance was calculated by means of Eq. (6), exhibiting a slight decrease as a function of the sweep rate for all the electrodes caused by the redox kinetic and/or electrolyte ionic diffusion limitations appearing at fast potential variations.
(6)
C =
∮ i
( V )
d V
2 s Π Δ V
In Eq. (6), s is the sweep rate, ΔV is the potential window (0.8 V), Π is the volume of analyzed material and the integral of the acquired current, i(V), is defined in the ΔV range. As observed in Fig. 5a, the as deposited film exhibits the lowest capacitance values, with a maximum of ca. 14 F/cm3 (@ 10 mV/s). Importantly, the thermal annealing generates a remarkable increase of the volumetric capacitance. The maximum capacitance measured at 10 mV/s, presented in Fig. 5b, reveals a parabolic trend with the annealing time at both temperatures (150 °C, 250 °C), being the development faster in the electrodes annealed at the highest temperature. The electrodes treated at 150 °C show a growing tendency, with very similar capacitance (around 51–53 F/cm3) for both annealing times (4 h, 6 h, respectively). However, with the highest temperature (250 °C), this maximum capacitance is reached in just 2 h of thermal treatment. Interestingly, the maximum capacitance is reached after 4 h of annealing (about 63 F/cm3), dropping down to about 27 F/cm3 after 6 h of thermal treatment. This behaviour is caused by the structural-compositional transformation of the material observed by XPS and Raman spectroscopy. Thermal annealing leads to the reduction of the rGO sheets and MWCNTs and their graphitic partial restitution, maintaining a low density of structural defects (Fig. 3) and enhancing their electric conductivity [66]. These processes are promoted by increasing the annealing time and temperature. Simultaneously, the oxidation of Ce2O3 and CeO2-x into stoichiometric CeO2 is driven, especially at 250 °C treatments (Fig. 3). Cerium dioxide is an abundant and inexpensive rare earth metal oxide with outstanding supercapacitive performance [67–72], so its formation upon annealing may contribute to the observed capacitance increase. Regarding the Mn oxides, the presence of MnO2, Mn2O3, Mn3O4 and MnO may contribute to the overall capacitance of the electrodes [73–78]. MnO2, that is commonly known as the Mn oxide with the best electrochemical performance, maintains practically unaltered its relative amount. However, the remarkable fade of the volumetric capacitance in the electrode treated at 250 °C during 6 h could be ascribed to the phase transformation of Mn2O3/Mn3O4 into Mn3O4/MnO (Fig. 3, Table 1), as well as the formation of a high amount of edge-like structural defects, linked to voids, in the nanocarbon entities leading to the deterioration of the sp2 CC conjugation domains and decline of effective surface area (Fig. 4, Table 2). It is worth mentioning that previous rGO-based hybrid electrodes deposited by RIMAPLE showed a wide range of capacitances, from 4 F/cm3 (@ 10 mV/s) of rGO-CNT-NiO electrodes to ca. 140 F/cm3 of rGO-CNT-CeOx-MnOx (ten times higher than the capacitance reported in this work) [42,43,46,50,79]. Therefore, a great improvement of the performance of such electrodes is expected after optimizing the thermal annealing process.

Galvanostatic charge-discharge experiments were also carried out to the electrodes in the 0.08–12 mA/cm2 current density range. Fig. 5c presents typical GCD plots of the as deposited electrode besides the one with the best performance (annealed at 250 °C during 4 h), and the corresponding volumetric capacitance was calculated for all the current densities by means of Eq. (7),
(7)
C =
I
t d
Π Δ V
where I is the applied current (modulus) during the measurement, and td is the discharge time. As observed, the GCD plots exhibit a triangle-shaped profile that indicates a quasi-linear potential response with time, which is characteristic of capacitive materials. The steep potential changes at the origin of charge/discharge processes in the as deposited electrode point to a large equivalent series resistance (ESR; ca. 280 Ω), much larger than the calculated ESR in the annealed electrode (14 Ω). Hence, the thermal annealing may lead to a decrease of the internal resistance, probably due to the reduction processes experienced in the rGO sheets and MWCNTs, that may enhance the power density of the electrode. Furthermore, the duration of the cycles of the annealed electrode (ca. 7s at 1 mA/cm2) is much longer than in the as deposited one (0.9s at 1 mA/cm2) pointing to a much larger capacitance (as expected). This fact is effectively observed in the plot of the volumetric capacitance as a function of the current density (Fig. 5c). The maximum capacitance obtained in the as deposited electrode is 9.4 F/cm3 (at 0.08 mA/cm2), whereas the annealed electrode exhibits 74.9 F/cm3 at the same current density (about 8-fold increase). Furthermore, the capacitance decreases with the applied current density caused by the ionic electromigration and the redox reactions kinetic limitations upon increasing currents, which produce higher potential in the outer surface of the electrode than in the inner material. The rate dependence on the charge/discharge rate was also analyzed through the Peukert's equation (Eq. 8) [80,81].
(8)

being C0, C and t0, t the respective capacitance and discharge time at two different discharge rates, and k the Peukert's constant. Note that k = 1 would denote ideal capacitance independency on the charge/discharge rate (i.e. no decrease of capacitance with current density). Accordingly, the higher k value is, the greater is the fade of the capacitance with the discharge rate (indicating loss of charge). Fig. 5d presents the volumetric capacitance as a function of the discharge time of the as deposited electrode and the one annealed during 4 h at 250 °C. The data fitted very well with the regression model based on Eq. (8) (determination coefficient R2 > 0.99), revealing a Peukert's constant of 1.49 and 1.28 for the as deposited and the annealed electrodes, respectively. The decrease of the k parameter attained with the thermal annealing could be mainly caused by the increase of electric conductivity of the carbon nanoentities upon their reduction and graphitization, which allow faster redistribution of the charge on the electrode surface. In addition, the annealed electrode contains a remarkably higher amount of CeO2-x/CeO2 than Ce2O3 (Fig. 3c), leading to an increase of the ionic conductivity that may promote the charge redistribution and storage [82] (probably, also boosted by faster redox kinetics).

Additional characterization of the fabricated electrodes was completed through EIS. The Nyquist plots are shown in Fig. 5e, exhibiting the typical outline of hybrid supercapacitor electrodes. In the high and middle frequencies range, interfacial electrostatic charge adsorption and faradaic reactions are dominant, creating the observed partial semicircle loop. At low frequencies, the ionic diffusion into the electrochemically active pores are the main contribution to the impedance, leading to a quasi-linear straight line. As observed, the profile of the plots significantly vary between the electrodes, even in the middle frequency region, indicating important kinetic/mass transport differences in the electromigration processes. All the curves were fitted using the modified Randles equivalent circuit represented in the inset of Fig. 5e, which is constituted by three branches with the following electric components: (i) two constant phase elements (CPE), related to non-ideal capacitance (caused by the material roughness, inhomogeneities, etc. at the solid/liquid interphase) [83], CPE1 represents the electric double layer (EDL) charge storage processes and CPE2 the pseudocapacitance contribution generated by the surface redox mechanisms; (ii) three resistances, R1 accounting for the ESR, R2 related to the charge-transfer resistance of the pseudocapacitive material and R3 representative of the charge leakages (non-controlled Faradaic reactions, charge redistribution, etc.); and (iii) one Warburg element (AW) for modelling the semi-infinite linear diffusion of the electrolyte ions [84]. The impedance of CPE and Warburg components are given by Eq. (9) and Eq. (10), respectively.

where j is the imaginary unit, ω is the angular frequency, Q is the CPE parameter related to the non-ideal capacitance, n is the constant phase accounting for ideality, and AW is the Warburg coefficient. A value of n = 1 denotes a system behaving as a pure capacitor, whereas n < 0.6 would indicate a high frequency dispersion. Table 3 summarizes the fitted values of the circuit elements parameters from the EIS data. As observed, R1 is similar in all the electrodes (about 11–13 Ω). Interestingly, the ESR, which is the sum of the intrinsic resistance of the electrodes and electrolyte, besides the electrode-electrolyte contact resistance, is similar to the one calculated from GCD of the electrode annealed during 4 h at 250 °C (14 Ω). However, in the as deposited electrode, there is a large difference between the values calculated by GCD (280 Ω) and EIS (12.7 Ω). It is worth mentioned that, for the galvanostatic method, the ESR calculation is based on the Ohm's law whereas the electrochemical systems encompassing electrode-electrolyte interfacial interactions, accumulation of charges and dispersion processes, are not well described by resistors and their behaviour will diverge from Ohm's law [85]. Consequently, the ESR value assessed by GCD would be overestimated (probably, it also includes the distributed resistance from the pores), and the one obtained by EIS would be more reliable for the description of the electrochemical system. The R2 (charge-transfer resistance) values are lower than 80 Ω in all the electrodes, exhibiting the lowest value (i.e. the fastest kinetics; 13 Ω) the electrodes annealed at 250 °C during 2 h and 4 h. The leakage resistance (R3) values are similar in all the electrodes. Concerning the CPEs, all the n values are higher in CPE2 than in CPE1. Moreover, the n values in as deposited and annealed electrodes at 150 °C are higher than their counterparts annealed at 250 °C (same annealing time). These results reveal almost ideal behaviour of CPE2 (associated to pseudocapacitance), and more dispersive mechanisms (especially of CPE1, related to EDL) after post-annealing at high temperature and longer dwell times, probably due to the creation of structural defects in the carbon material (rGO, MWCNTs). Note that the Q values, especially Q2 which is related to the pseudocapacitance, remarkably increase with the annealing process (formation of CeO2-x besides Mn3O4/MnO at the expense of, respectively, Ce2O3 and Mn2O3). Finally, remarking that the lowest Warburg coefficient (the fastest mass-transport) is obtained in the electrode exhibiting the highest capacitance (4h-250 °C), whereas the highest coefficient (the most sluggish ionic transport) is revealed in the electrode with the highest density of structural defects (6h-250 °C), followed by the as deposited electrode.

In this work, the deposition of hybrid electrodes composed of rGO and MWCNTs coated with a plethora of cerium oxides (Ce2O3, CeO2), and manganese oxides (MnO, Mn3O4, Mn2O3, MnO2) nanoparticles was accomplished by using GO sheets, MWCNTs, CeO2 NPs and, for the first time, the (NH4)2[Mn2(C6H5O7)2(H2O)2] coordination complex precursor in the RIMAPLE target. The use of the coordination compound allowed obtaining α-MnO2 and CeO2-x which are appropriate oxide phases for achieving high values of pseudocapacitance in the electrode. Additionally, mild post-annealing processes in air were proven to induce the deoxygenation of the carbon nanoentities, as well as the oxidation of the CeOx species (Ce2O3 into CeO2-x) and the reduction of Mn2O3/Mn3O4 into Mn3O4/MnO while maintaining the amount of MnO2. These key structural-compositional alterations considerably enhanced the energy storage performance of the electrodes. The highest capacitance increase, along with the fastest redox kinetics and mass-transport, were obtained by annealing the deposited electrode at 250 °C during 4 h. A longer heat treatment leads to a worsening of the electrochemical performance (decline of volumetric capacitance, more sluggish redox kinetics and mass transport) caused by the substantial increase of structural defects in the carbon framework, as well as the more extended reduction of the Mn2O3/Mn3O4 phases into Mn3O4/MnO.
